Now that we've written some copy that hopefully you're going to go home, you're going to go back to the office, you're going to take and try to make it better, how do you make copy better? I think it was David Ogilvy that said, you know, I'm really not a very good writer, but I'm really good at making copy better. So he said, all I do is I just write and yeah, it's not so good, but I'm really good at making it better. So I make it better and better and better and better, and then it becomes great David Ogilvy copywriting. Another thing he said, by the way, is that David Ogilvy would always do, he would give his copy to people, he would say, here, make this better, make this better. He didn't want to hear how good it was, he didn't want to hear their opinion, just make it better. Tell me what I can do to make it better. And you know, it's something that I've always found, I think we were talking about it, maybe it was this group, maybe it was the other group. People who never become very good copywriters, people who are always okay copywriters, invariably they're the people that you can't really tell them this was boring, this copy's not good, here's what you could do to make it better. The great copywriter is John Carlton, he'll give me copy, I'll go John, this is crap. He'll go, yes Dave, I sort of thought that in the back of my head, you're right, I'm going to take it, and he'll go and he'll make it so it's not crap anymore. And he loves to hear, I mean he doesn't love to hear this crap, but he loves to hear what he needs to do to make it better. And that's the way that we all need to be, it's like thank you, thank you for telling me this is boring, thank you for telling me you don't believe me. The people that read your copy, you want to really encourage them to do that. You know, don't tell me it's good, you can tell me anything, I don't care. Tell me it's boring, tell me it's not believable, that's what's going to help me make it better. So making it better, read your copy out loud. It's really hard to do, I always try to find a way to skip doing this, but it's so different, when you take your copy and you go, dear dad, blah blah blah blah blah blah blah blah You hear things that you don't see when you're just reading it. You know, you hear which copy, which paragraphs sing, which paragraphs have energy, you hear where it gets boring. If you can, read it to someone else. That's even better, is to read your copy to someone else. Have someone else read your copy. Boy, that's an experience. Here, read this to me. And you know, they start reading, you know, ah, dah dah dah dah dah dah dah dah dah dah and you start going, oh my god, well this never ends. You know, you don't want to feel that way. You want them to read your copy and you go, yeah, I would be interested in that. Yeah, yeah, that doesn't bore me. Oh, that part there, right there, bored me. I got to make a note that I got to change that, right? I got to put something in there to wake the reader up. This is the way you make copy better, you know, not by sitting in your office or by your computer going, oh, I should, maybe I'll move this here, maybe I'll change this word. You can underestimate sometimes, I think, how simple copy needs to be, right? People are reading this stuff. They're not reading it with a highlighter. They haven't shut their internet off. They haven't turned off their cell phone. They haven't isolated themselves in a room where they won't be interrupted. They're in the middle of life. Their kids are screaming. Their husbands and wives want dinner or whatever. And the office, the office, the phones are ringing for the business to business. They're not studying it very carefully. So if it's not caveman simple, then they're not going to be able to understand it. They're not going to be able to order. So one great way to improve copy is make it as simple as you can. That doesn't mean you use simple words. It doesn't mean you dumb it down, right? But it means, look how successful Donald Trump was. A lot of his success was just being simple. Just saying things in a simple way, right? He never said, oh, I'm going to look at, like Hillary would say, oh, I'm going to look at the study of the problem of immigration. And we're going to be much more careful about who we let in and who we don't. But we're going to welcome all people. And I'm going to point the committee that's going to study the situation. And they're going to make recommendations. And then I'm going to implement those recommendations. Donald Trump just said, we're going to build a wall. We're going to make Mexico pay for it. Boom. Everybody understands, right? Smart, not smart, rich, poor. Everybody understands I'm going to build a wall. And I'm going to make Mexico pay for it. Of course, is he building the wall? Is he making Mexico pay for it? No. But it made a great campaign promise. Hopefully, we want to say things that we're actually going to do and that are true. But you can see the power from that, right? See the power from his simple language. Look at his speeches. He talks with simple words. It's amazing. It's fantastic. People call me every day and tell me they love it. He uses such simple words, simple sentences. People get bogged down in our long sentences that we write. Great way to improve copy. You just make it more simple and clear. And as Simon said too, at a certain point, good enough is good enough, right? You've got to know. It's another thing you've got to know. You've got to know where that point is, where you get it to the 80-20 rule. Everybody go to the 80-20 rule, right? It's the same thing with copy. You don't want to 80%, or it's going to get you your top 20% of results. To get it to the next 20% could take you hours and hours and hours and hours and hours. Is it really worth it, or is it better to just write a different promotion? So don't struggle to get, and I know because I do it, don't struggle to get that last 10%, that last 5%, that last little bit of perfection. Know when it's good enough, send it out. One last thing I wanted to tell you about, about copy, one last trick. When you read your copy, you know how it is sometimes when you go, oh, that was a great paragraph. I like that paragraph, right? So you got to listen to yourself. And when you find the paragraph that's a great paragraph, you got to go back to all the other paragraphs and say, how can I make these other paragraphs come up to that level, right? You want to move everything up to the best of your copy. And the best of your copy, I think you're going to find, is where the copy has energy, where the copy has life. You've got to develop a feeling for that, a feeling for where in your copy it has life. You know how some of the copy we read, and it's especially a trap, of course, if you're talking about economics and finance, and this is going to happen, and the government's going to do this, and it's got no life. It's got no energy. And then all of a sudden, someone's talking about their father, and it's, you know, oh, that woke me up, because that's got life. That's got energy. Someone's talking about, they hate each other, and they're at odds with each other, and there's going to be banging of heads and explosions. Oh, that's got energy. So try to get all your copy up to that level. A lot of times I think people write things that are just a way of getting from this point to this point, right? Great copy never has anything in it that is only for the purpose of getting someone from this point to this point, right? It's not just to get them to there. What's in between is also great life, energy, selling, proving, explaining copy, right? It's not just, well, I got to stay this. I got to, a lot of times copyrighters will say to me, I'll say, why did you write this part here, and they, oh, I had to explain to the reader, blah, blah, blah. You know? It's like your need to explain something is not the reader's need to read it. That's like really important, right? Just because we need to tell, just because I need to tell you that this diabetes cure works by doing such and such in order to do such so that the little cells don't make too much sugar that gives you the diabetes or whatever it is. That doesn't mean the reader needs to read it. It doesn't mean the reader wants to read it. So it's got to be, I've got to have more purpose than just I need to tell them so that I can explain how my product does it better, and that's why you should buy my product. I've got to make it so exciting and so interesting for the reader about these cells, and these cells don't make enough insulin. And so these other cells come and fight with them. And before you know it, all the fighting leads to inflammation. And it's this big conflagration in your body. And I've got to work so hard to make that exciting. Can't just be putting words on paper. So that's how you want to read your copy, right? Is with that. Nope, nope. It's not good enough. Got to make that better. Got to make that more exciting. Got to have people killing each other. Got to have things exploding, right? Do the warriors. I mean, look at that. Even the look of that copy was, you know, I mean, the words were great too. But, you know, we could see just scrolling down. So many exciting things happen. And that's how your copy has to be. You know, that three-ring circle. Look, what's that? Zulu warriors are coming here. You know, exciting things are happening. People are fighting each other. They're hating the government. They're making auditors be embarrassed. They're having victories. Their grandchildren love them again. There's all sorts of things. That was the most interesting copy we had. Any time there was someone doing something. Any time there was someone losing photographs and being upset. Any time there was, you know, auditors being hated, the government being hated. Any time the drug companies. Any time there was struggle. Someone was sick. That was what gets our attention. That's what gets our readers' attention. So always make sure your copy's got people doing stuff.
